The **Tiger I** (German: [[ˈtiːɡɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Tiger-pronunciation.ogg "File:De-Tiger-pronunciation.ogg")) was a [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") [heavy tank](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") that began operational duty in 1942 in [Africa](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign") and in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), usually in independent [heavy tank battalions](/wiki/German_heavy_tank_battalion "German heavy tank battalion"). It gave the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(1935%E2%80%931945) "German Army (1935–1945)") its first armoured fighting vehicle that mounted the [8.8 cm (3.5 in) KwK 36](/wiki/8.8_cm_KwK_36 "8.8 cm KwK 36") gun (derived from the [8.8 cm Flak 36](/wiki/8.8_cm_Flak_18/36/37/41 "8.8 cm Flak 18/36/37/41"), the famous "eighty-eight" feared by Allied troops). 1,347 were built between August 1942 and August 1944.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrewhitt199926-16) After August 1944, production of the Tiger I was phased out in favour of the [Tiger II](/wiki/Tiger_II "Tiger II").

While the Tiger I has been called an outstanding design for its time,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBishop20029-17) it has also been criticized for being [overengineered](/wiki/Overengineering "Overengineering"),[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker-Jones20127-18) and for using expensive materials and labour-intensive production methods. In the early period, the Tiger was prone to certain types of track failures and breakdowns. It was expensive to maintain, but generally mechanically reliable.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrabkinSheremet200643-19) It was difficult to transport and vulnerable to immobilisation when mud, ice, and snow froze between its overlapping and interleaved *Schachtellaufwerk*-pattern road wheels, often jamming them solid.

The tank was given its nickname "Tiger" by the ministry for armament and ammunition by 7 August 1941,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199713-20) and the [Roman numeral](/wiki/Roman_numeral "Roman numeral") was added after the Tiger II entered production. It was classified with [ordnance inventory designation](/wiki/Sonderkraftfahrzeug "Sonderkraftfahrzeug") *Sd.Kfz. 182*. The tank was later re-designated as **Panzerkampfwagen VI Ausführung E** (abbreviated as **Pz.Kpfw. VI Ausf. E**) in March 1943, with ordnance inventory designation ***Sd.Kfz. 181***.

Today, only nine Tiger I tanks survive in museums and private collections worldwide. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tiger_I&action=edit), [Tiger 131](/wiki/Tiger_131 "Tiger 131") (captured during the [North African campaign](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign")) at the UK's [Tank Museum](/wiki/Bovington_Tank_Museum "Bovington Tank Museum") is the only example restored to running order.

## Development

### Initial development

Combat experience against the French [SOMUA S35](/wiki/SOMUA_S35 "SOMUA S35") [cavalry tank](/wiki/Cavalry_tank "Cavalry tank") and [Char B1](/wiki/Char_B1 "Char B1") heavy tank, and the British [Matilda II](/wiki/Matilda_II "Matilda II") [infantry tanks](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank") during the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") in June 1940 showed that the German Army needed better armed and armoured tanks.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200812–13-21)

On 26 May 1941, Henschel and Ferdinand Porsche were asked to submit designs for a 45-tonne heavy tank, to be ready by June 1942.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200813-22) [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") worked on an updated version of their [VK 30.01 (P)](/wiki/VK_30.01_(P) "VK 30.01 (P)") Leopard tank prototype while Henschel worked on an improved VK 36.01 (H) tank. Henschel built two prototypes: a [VK 45.01 (H)](/wiki/VK_45.01_(H) "VK 45.01 (H)") H1 with an 8.8 cm L/56 cannon, and a VK 45.01 (H) H2 with a 7.5 cm L/70 cannon.

### Final designs

On 22 June 1941, Germany launched [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), the invasion of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). The Germans encountered large numbers of Soviet [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") medium and [KV-1](/wiki/KV-1_(tank) "KV-1 (tank)") heavy tanks.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200814-23) According to Henschel designer [Erwin Aders](/wiki/Erwin_Aders "Erwin Aders"), "There was great consternation when it was discovered that the Soviet tanks were superior to anything available to the [Heer](/wiki/German_Army_(1935%E2%80%931945) "German Army (1935–1945)")."[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarruthers2013chapt._"Design_features"-24)

Weight increase to 45 tonnes and an increase in gun calibre to 8.8 cm (3.5 in) were ordered for it on 26 May 1941.[[21]](#cite_note-25) The due date for the new prototypes was set for 20 April 1942, [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s 53rd birthday. Unlike the [Panther tank](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank"), the designs did not incorporate [sloped armour](/wiki/Sloped_armour "Sloped armour").

Porsche and Henschel submitted prototype designs, each making use of the Krupp-designed turret. They were demonstrated at [Rastenburg](/wiki/Rastenburg "Rastenburg") in front of Hitler. The Henschel design was accepted, mainly because the Porsche [VK 4501 (P)](/wiki/VK_4501_(P) "VK 4501 (P)") prototype design used a troubled [petrol-electric transmission system](/wiki/Petrol%E2%80%93electric_transmission "Petrol–electric transmission") which needed large quantities of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") for the manufacture of its electrical drivetrain components, a strategic war material of which Germany had limited supplies with acceptable electrical properties for such uses.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle19936-26) Production of the *Panzerkampfwagen* VI *Ausf.* H began in August 1942. Expecting an order for his tank, Porsche built 100 chassis. After the contract was awarded to Henschel, they were used for a new turretless, [casemate](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate")-style [tank destroyer](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"); 91 hulls were converted into the [*Panzerjäger* Tiger (P)](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant") in early 1943.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-278-0873-24%2C_Russland%2C_Panzersoldat_%28Oberleutnant_Knauth_-%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-278-0873-24%2C_Russland%2C_Panzersoldat_%28Oberleutnant_Knauth_-%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-278-0873-24,_Russland,_Panzersoldat_(Oberleutnant_Knauth_-).jpg)

Early Tiger with tall cupola

The Tiger was still at the prototype stage when it was first hurried into service, and therefore changes both large and small were made throughout the production run. A redesigned turret with a lower cupola was the most significant change. The river-fording submersion capability and an external air-filtration system were dropped to cut costs.[[23]](#cite_note-27)

## Design

The Tiger differed from earlier [German tanks](/wiki/Tanks_in_the_German_Army "Tanks in the German Army") principally in its design philosophy. Its predecessors balanced mobility, [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour") and [firepower](/wiki/Firepower "Firepower") and were sometimes outgunned by their opponents.

While heavy, this tank was not slower than the best of its opponents. Although the general design and layout were broadly similar to the [Panzer IV](/wiki/Panzer_IV "Panzer IV") medium tank, the Tiger weighed more than twice as much. This was due to its substantially thicker armour, the larger [main gun](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun"), greater volume of fuel and ammunition storage, larger engine, and a more solidly built transmission and suspension.

### Armour

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-299-1805-10%2C_Nordfrankreich%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-299-1805-10%2C_Nordfrankreich%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-299-1805-10,_Nordfrankreich,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I).jpg)

The Tiger I's [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour") was up to 200 mm on the [gun mantlet](/wiki/Gun_mantlet "Gun mantlet").

The Tiger I had frontal hull armour 100 mm (3.9 in) thick, frontal [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") of 100 mm and [gun mantlet](/wiki/Gun_mantlet "Gun mantlet") with a varying thickness of 120 to 200 mm (4.7 to 7.9 in).[[24]](#cite_note-id3486.securedata.net-28) The Tiger had 60 mm (2.4 in) thick hull side plates and 80 mm (3.1 in) armour on the side superstructure/sponsons, while turret sides and rear were 80 mm. The top and bottom armour was 25 mm (1 in) thick; from March 1944, the turret roof was thickened to 40 mm (1.6 in).[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle19938,_16-10) Armour plates were mostly flat, with interlocking construction. This flat construction encouraged angling the Tiger hull roughly 30-45° when firing in order to increase effective thickness.

### Gun

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Turmzielfernrohr_9c.jpg/250px-Turmzielfernrohr_9c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turmzielfernrohr_9c.jpg)

*Turmzielfernrohr* TZF 9c gun sight

Main article: [8.8 cm KwK 36](/wiki/8.8_cm_KwK_36 "8.8 cm KwK 36")

The 56-calibre long 8.8 cm KwK 36 was chosen for the Tiger. A combination of a flat trajectory from the high muzzle velocity and precision from the Leitz *Turmzielfernrohr* [TZF 9b sight](/w/index.php?title=TZF_9&action=edit&redlink=1 "TZF 9 (page does not exist)") (later replaced by the [monocular](/wiki/Monocular "Monocular") TZF 9c) made it very accurate. In British wartime firing trials, five successive hits were scored on a 16 by 18 in (410 by 460 mm) target at a range of 1,200 yards (1,100 m).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarruthers2013chapt._"Design_features"-24) Compared with the other contemporary German tank guns, the 8.8 cm KwK 36 had superior penetration to the [7.5 cm KwK 40](/wiki/7.5_cm_KwK_40 "7.5 cm KwK 40") on the [Sturmgeschütz III](/wiki/Sturmgesch%C3%BCtz_III "Sturmgeschütz III") and [Panzer IV](/wiki/Panzer_IV "Panzer IV") but inferior to the [7.5 cm KwK 42](/wiki/7.5_cm_KwK_42 "7.5 cm KwK 42") on the [Panther tank](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank")[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirdLivingston200161-29) under ranges of 2,500 metres. At greater ranges, the 8.8 cm KwK 36 was superior in penetration and accuracy. British trials found the gun took from 6 to 16 seconds to reload varying on turret position and consequently which storage bin was being used.[[26]](#cite_note-30)

The ammunition for the Tiger had electrically fired primers. Four types of ammunition were available but not all were fully available; the PzGr 40 shell used tungsten, which was in short supply as the war progressed.

* *[PzGr. 39](/wiki/Panzergranate_39 "Panzergranate 39")* ([armour-piercing, capped, ballistic cap](/wiki/Armour-piercing,_capped,_ballistic_cap "Armour-piercing, capped, ballistic cap"))
* *PzGr.* 40 ([armour-piercing, composite rigid](/wiki/Armour-piercing,_composite_rigid "Armour-piercing, composite rigid"))
* *Hl. Gr.* 39 ([high explosive anti-tank](/wiki/High_explosive_anti-tank "High explosive anti-tank"))
* *sch. Sprgr. Patr.* L/4.5 ([incendiary](/wiki/Incendiary_device "Incendiary device") [shrapnel](/wiki/Shrapnel_shell "Shrapnel shell"))

### Engine and drive

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2936-27%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Wartung.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2936-27%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Wartung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2936-27,_Russland,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I),_Wartung.jpg)

Crew working on the engine through the hatch on the rear hull roof

The rear of the tank held an engine compartment flanked by two separate rear compartments each containing a fuel tank and radiator. The Germans had not developed an adequate diesel engine, so a petrol (gasoline) powerplant had to be used instead. The original engine used was a 21.35 L (1,303 in3) 12-cylinder [Maybach](/wiki/Maybach "Maybach") [HL210 P45](/wiki/List_of_WWII_Maybach_engines "List of WWII Maybach engines") developing 485 kW (650 hp) at 3,000 rpm and a top speed of 38 km/h (24 mph).[[27]](#cite_note-31) It was found to be underpowered for the vehicle from the 251st Tiger onwards. It was replaced by the upgraded [HL 230](/wiki/Maybach_HL230 "Maybach HL230") P45, a 23.095 L (1,409 in3) engine developing 521 kW (699 hp) at 3,000 rpm.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200841-32) The main difference between these engines was that the original Maybach HL 210 used an aluminium engine block while the Maybach HL 230 used a cast-iron engine block. The cast-iron block allowed for larger cylinders (and thus, greater displacement) which increased the power output to 521 kW (699 hp). The engine was in V-form, with two cylinder banks set at 60 degrees. An [inertia starter](/wiki/Aircraft_engine_starting#Inertia_starter "Aircraft engine starting") was mounted on its right side, driven via chain gears through a port in the rear wall. The engine could be lifted out through a hatch on the rear hull roof. In comparison to other V12 and various vee-form gasoline engines used for tanks, the eventual HL 230 engine was nearly 4 L (240 in3) smaller in displacement than the Allied British [Rolls-Royce Meteor](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Meteor "Rolls-Royce Meteor") V12 AFV power plant, itself adapted from the RR Merlin but de-rated to 448 kW (601 hp) power output; and the American Ford-designed precursor V12 to its [Ford GAA](/wiki/Ford_GAA_engine "Ford GAA engine") V-8 AFV engine of 18 litre displacement, which in its original V12 form would have had the same 27 L (1,600 in3) displacement as the Meteor.

The [501st Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/501st_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "501st Heavy Panzer Battalion") (*sPzAbt 501*) reported in May 1943:

> …Regarding the overheating engines, the HL 210 engine caused no troubles during the recent time. All occurring breakdowns resulted from the low quality of driver training. In several cases engine failures have to be put down to the missing remote engine thermometer. Five engines have reached more than 3,000 km without essential failures. A good driver is essential for the successful deployment of the Tiger, he must have a good technical training and has to keep his nerve in critical situations…[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201756–57-33)[[30]](#cite_note-34)

The engine drove the front sprockets through a drivetrain connecting to a transmission in the front portion of the lower hull; the front sprockets had to be mounted relatively low as a result. The Krupp-designed 11-tonne turret had a hydraulic motor whose pump was powered by mechanical drive from the engine. A full rotation took about a minute.

Another new feature was the Maybach-Olvar [hydraulically controlled](/wiki/Hydraulic_machinery "Hydraulic machinery") semi-automatic [pre-selector gearbox](/wiki/Pre-selector_gearbox "Pre-selector gearbox"). The extreme weight of the tank also required a new steering system. Germany's [Argus Motoren](/wiki/Argus_Motoren "Argus Motoren"), where Hermann Klaue had invented a [ring brake](/w/index.php?title=Ring_brake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ring brake (page does not exist)")[[31]](#cite_note-35) in 1940, supplied them for the [Arado Ar 96](/wiki/Arado_Ar_96 "Arado Ar 96")[[32]](#cite_note-36) and also supplied the 55 cm (22 in) disc.[[33]](#cite_note-37) Klaue was acknowledged in the patent application that he had improved, it can even be traced back to British designs dating to 1904. It is unclear whether Klaue's patent ring brake was used in the Tiger brake design.

The clutch-and-brake system, typical for lighter vehicles, was retained only for emergencies. Normally, steering depended on a double [differential](/wiki/Differential_(mechanical_device) "Differential (mechanical device)"), Henschel's development of the British [Merritt-Brown](/wiki/David_Brown_Ltd. "David Brown Ltd.") system[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200844-38) first encountered in the [Churchill tank](/wiki/Churchill_tank "Churchill tank"). The vehicle had an eight-speed gearbox, and the steering offered two fixed radii of turns on each gear, thus the Tiger had sixteen different radii of turn. In first gear, at a speed of a few km/h, the minimal turning radius was 3.44 m (11 ft 3 in). In neutral gear, the tracks could be turned in opposite directions, so the Tiger I pivoted in place.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200846-39) There was a steering wheel instead of either a tiller – or, as most tanks had at that time, twin braking levers – making the Tiger I's steering system easy to use, and ahead of its time.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200844-38)

Powered turret traverse was provided by the variable speed Boehringer-Sturm L4 hydraulic motor, which was driven from the main engine by a secondary drive shaft. On early production versions of the Tiger maximum turret traverse was limited to 6 degrees per second, whilst on later versions a selectable high speed traverse gear was added. Thus, the turret could be rotated 360 degrees at up to 6 degrees per second in low gear independent of engine rpm (same as on early production versions), or up to 19 degrees per second with the high-speed setting and engine at 2,000 rpm, and at over 36 degrees per second at the maximum allowable engine speed of 3,000 rpm. The direction and speed of traverse was controlled by the gunner through foot pedals, the speed of traverse corresponding to the level of depression the gunner applied to the foot pedal. This system allowed for very precise control of powered traverse, a light touch on the pedal resulting in a minimum traverse speed of 0.1 degrees per second (360 degrees in 60 minutes), unlike in most other tanks of the time (e.g., the US M4 Sherman or Soviet T-34) this allowed for fine laying of the gun without the gunner needing to use his traverse handwheel.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarius200323-40)

### Suspension

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-28%2C_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-28%2C_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-28,_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg)

Clear view of the Tiger I's *Schachtellaufwerk* overlapping and interleaved road wheels during production

The suspension used sixteen [torsion bars](/wiki/Torsion_bar "Torsion bar"), with eight suspension arms per side. To save space, the swing arms were leading on one side and trailing on the other side; this is called an H suspension[[37]](#cite_note-41) setup. There were three road wheels (one of them double, closest to the track's centre) on each arm, in a so-called *Schachtellaufwerk* overlapping and interleaved arrangement, similar to that pioneered on German half-tracked military vehicles of the pre-World War II era, with the Tiger I being the first all-tracked German AFV built in quantity to use such a road wheel arrangement. The wheels had a diameter of 800 mm (31 in) in the *Schachtellaufwerk* arrangement for the Tiger I's suspension, providing a high uniform distribution of the load onto the track, at the cost of increased maintenance.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-310-0899-15%2C_Italien%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Reparatur.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-310-0899-15%2C_Italien%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Reparatur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-310-0899-15,_Italien,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I),_Reparatur.jpg)

Wheel and track maintenance in muddy conditions

Removing an inner wheel that had lost its solid rubber tire (a common occurrence) required the removal of up to nine other wheels first. During the rainy period that brought on the autumn *[rasputitsa](/wiki/Rasputitsa "Rasputitsa")* mud season and onwards into the winter conditions on the [Eastern front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_of_World_War_II "Eastern Front of World War II"), the roadwheels of a *Schachtellaufwerk*-equipped vehicle could also become packed with mud or snow that could then freeze.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Presumably, German engineers, based on the experience of the half-tracks, felt that the improvement in off-road performance, track and wheel life, mobility with wheels missing or damaged, plus additional protection from enemy fire was worth the maintenance difficulties of a complex system vulnerable to mud and ice. This approach was carried on, in various forms, to the [Panther](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank") and the non-interleaved wheel design for the [Tiger II](/wiki/Tiger_II "Tiger II"). Eventually, a new 80 cm diameter 'steel' wheel design with an internally sprung steel-rim tire was substituted. As these new wheels could carry more weight, the outermost wheel on each suspension arm was removed. The same wheels would also be used on the Tiger II.[[38]](#cite_note-:0-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61%2C_Kassel%2C_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61%2C_Kassel%2C_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61,_Kassel,_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)

Tiger at the [Henschel plant](/wiki/Henschel_%26_Son#World_War_II "Henschel & Son") is loaded onto a special rail car. The outer road wheels have been removed and narrow tracks put in place to decrease vehicle width, allowing it to fit within the [loading gauge](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge") of the German rail network.

To support the considerable weight of the Tiger, the tracks were 725 mm (28.5 in) wide. To meet [rail-freight size restrictions](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge"), narrower 520 mm (20 in) wide 'transport' tracks (*Verladeketten*) could be installed.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle200036-43)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200844-38)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilbeck2004-44) For Tigers equipped with rubber-tired wheels, this also required the outermost roadwheel on each axle (16 total) to be removed.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle200035-45)[[38]](#cite_note-:0-42) The track replacement and wheel removal took 30 minutes for each side of the tank.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201369-46) However, in service, Tigers were frequently transported by rail with their combat tracks fitted, as long as the train crew knew there were no narrow tunnels or other obstructions on the route that would prevent an oversized load from passing, despite this practice being strictly forbidden.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201371-47)

### Fording system

The Tiger tank's combat weight of 56 tons was often too heavy for small bridges which had 35 ton weight limits, so it was designed to ford bodies of water up to 5 m (15 ft) deep.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200820-9) This required unusual mechanisms for ventilation and cooling when underwater. At least 30 minutes of set-up time was required, with the turret and gun being locked in the forward position, and a large [snorkel](/wiki/Vehicle_snorkel "Vehicle snorkel") tube raised at the rear. An inflatable doughnut-shaped ring sealed the turret ring. The two rear compartments (each containing a fuel tank, radiator and fans) were floodable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, this ability was found to be of limited practical value for its high cost and was removed from production lines in August 1943.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown200820-9)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForty2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidLsxJHQFbPSgCpgPA51_51]-48) As a result, only the first 495 units were fitted with this [deep fording](/wiki/Deep_fording "Deep fording") system;[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForty2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidLsxJHQFbPSgCpgPA51_51]-48) all later models were capable of fording water only two metres deep.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Crew compartment

The internal layout was typical of German tanks. Forward was an open crew compartment, with the driver and radio-operator seated at the front on either side of the gearbox. Behind them the turret floor was surrounded by panels forming a continuous level surface. This helped the loader to retrieve the ammunition, which was mostly stowed above the tracks. Three men were seated in the turret; the loader to the right of the gun facing to the rear, the gunner to the left of the gun, and the commander behind him. There was also a folding seat on the right for the loader. The turret had a full circular floor and 157 cm (62 in) headroom. Early versions of the Tiger I's turret included two [pistol ports](/wiki/Firing_port "Firing port"); however, one of these was replaced with a loader escape hatch and the other was removed from later designs.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenBrown2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidS1gbrYELbT4CpgPA88_88]-49)

Post-war testing by the Allies found the tank to be uncomfortable and spartan. For example, the gunner suffered from clumsy controls and a cramped area.[[46]](#cite_note-50) This was in contrast to German crews who found them to be spacious and comfortable.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForty2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidLsxJHQFbPSgCpgPA107_108]-51)

### Cost

A substantial problem with the Tiger was that its production required considerable resources in terms of manpower and material, which led to it being expensive: the Tiger I cost over twice as much as a Panzer IV and four times as much as a [StuG III](/wiki/Sturmgesch%C3%BCtz_III "Sturmgeschütz III") [assault gun](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun").[[48]](#cite_note-52) Partly because of their high cost, only 1,347 Tiger I and 492 Tiger II tanks were produced.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentz1996288-53) The closest counterpart to the Tiger from the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was the [M26 Pershing](/wiki/M26_Pershing "M26 Pershing") (around 200 deployed to the [European Theater of Operations](/wiki/European_Theater_of_Operations "European Theater of Operations") (ETO) during the war[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunnicutt1971-54)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]) and the [IS-2](/wiki/Iosif_Stalin_tank "Iosif Stalin tank") from the USSR (about 3,800 built during the conflict).

From a technical point of view, it was better than its contemporaries.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga2015204-55) Despite the low number produced, shortages in qualified crew and the considerable fuel consumption in the face of ever shrinking resources, Tigers (including Tiger IIs) destroyed at least 10,300 enemy tanks and 11,380 anti-tank guns and artillery pieces for the loss of 1,725 Tigers[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] (including large numbers of operational and strategic losses, i.e. abandoned, broken down, etc.).[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAskey2017103-56)

## Production history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-05%2C_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-05%2C_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-635-3965-05,_Panzerfabrik_in_Deutschland.jpg)

Installing the turret

Production of the Tiger I began in August 1942 at the factory of [Henschel und Sohn](/wiki/Henschel_%26_Son "Henschel & Son") in [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"),[[53]](#cite_note-57) initially at a rate of 25 per month and peaking in April 1944 at 104 per month. An official document of the time stated that the first Tiger I was completed on 4 August. 1,355 had been built by August 1944, when production ceased. Deployed Tiger I's peaked at 671 on 1 July 1944.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199313-58) It took about twice as long to build a Tiger I as another German tank of the period. When the improved Tiger II began production in January 1944, the Tiger I was soon phased out.

In 1943, Japan bought several specimens of German tank designs for study. A single Tiger I was apparently purchased, along with a Panther and two Panzer IIIs, but only the Panzer IIIs were actually delivered.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga200717-59) The undelivered Tiger was loaned to the German Wehrmacht by the Japanese government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Czo%C5%82g_niemiecki_PzKpfw_VI_%22Tiger%22_w_greckim_miasteczku_%282-600%29.jpg/250px-Czo%C5%82g_niemiecki_PzKpfw_VI_%22Tiger%22_w_greckim_miasteczku_%282-600%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Czo%C5%82g_niemiecki_PzKpfw_VI_%22Tiger%22_w_greckim_miasteczku_(2-600).jpg)

Tiger I Ausf. H1 covered in Zimmerit anti-magnetic mine paste in Greece, 1944.

Many modifications were introduced during the production run to improve automotive performance, firepower and protection. Simplification of the design was implemented, along with cuts due to raw material shortages. In 1942 alone, at least six revisions were made, starting with the removal of the *Vorpanzer* (frontal armour shield) from the pre-production models in April. In May, mudguards bolted onto the side of the pre-production run were added, while removable mudguards saw full incorporation in September. Smoke discharge canisters, three on each side of the turret, were added in August 1942. In later years, similar changes and updates were added, such as the addition of [Zimmerit](/wiki/Zimmerit "Zimmerit") (a non-magnetic anti-mine coating), in late 1943.[[56]](#cite_note-60)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrawford200041-61)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199312-62) Due to slow production rates at the factories, incorporation of the new modifications could take several months.

The humorous and somewhat racy crew manual, the *[Tigerfibel](/wiki/Tigerfibel "Tigerfibel")*, was the first of its kind for the German Army and its success resulted in more unorthodox manuals that attempted to emulate its style.

## Variants

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-277-0846-10%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29_b.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-277-0846-10%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-277-0846-10,_Russland,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I)_b.jpg)

Tiger Tank in snow

Among other variants of the Tiger, a heavily armoured [casemate](/wiki/Casemate#Armoured_vehicles "Casemate") self-propelled rocket projector, today commonly known as the *[Sturmtiger](/wiki/Sturmtiger "Sturmtiger")*, was built, which mounted a 38 cm (15 in) rocket launcher.[[59]](#cite_note-63) A tank recovery version of the Porsche Tiger I ([Bergetiger](/wiki/Bergetiger "Bergetiger")), and one Porsche Tiger I, was issued to the 654th Heavy Tank Destroyer Battalion, which was equipped with the Ferdinand/[Elefant](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant").
In Italy, a demolition carrier version of the Tiger I without a main gun was built by maintenance crews in an effort to find a way to clear minefields. It is often misidentified as a *BergeTiger* recovery vehicle. As many as three may have been built. It carried a demolition charge on a small crane on the turret in place of the main gun. It was to move up to a minefield and drop the charge, back away, and then set the charge off to clear the minefield. There is no verification of any being used in combat.

Another variant was the [Fahrschulpanzer](/w/index.php?title=Fahrschulpanzer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fahrschulpanzer (page does not exist)") VI Tiger tanks (driving school Tiger tanks). These tanks were Tigers with modified engines to run on either compressed [Towngas](/wiki/Towngas "Towngas") gas (Stadtgas System) or [wood gas](/wiki/Wood_gas "Wood gas") (Holzgas System). This was due to shortages in fuel supply. They used a mixture of turreted and turretless hulls. They were used to train Tiger tank crews, and were not used in combat.

## Combat history

### Gun and armour performance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-24%2C_Russland%2C_Treffer_an_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-24%2C_Russland%2C_Treffer_an_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-24,_Russland,_Treffer_an_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I).jpg)

German soldiers inspect a non-penetrating hit to the Tiger's armour.

A report prepared by the [*Waffenamt-Prüfwesen* 1](/wiki/Waffenamt "Waffenamt") gave the calculated probability of perforation at range, on which various adversaries would be defeated reliably at a side angle of 30 degrees to the incoming round.

The *Wa Prüf* report estimated that the Tiger's 88 mm gun would be capable of penetrating the differential case of an American [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") from 2,100 m (1.3 mi) and the turret front from 1,800 m (1.1 mi), but the Tiger's 88 mm gun would not penetrate the upper glacis plate at any range assuming a side angle of 30 degrees. The M4 Sherman's 75 mm gun would not penetrate the Tiger frontally at any range, and needed to be within 100 m (300 ft) to achieve a side penetration against the 80 mm upper hull superstructure. The Sherman's upgraded [76 mm gun](/wiki/76_mm_gun_M1 "76 mm gun M1") might penetrate the Tiger's driver's front plate from 600 m (2,000 ft), the nose from 400 m (1,300 ft) and the turret front from 700 m (2,300 ft).[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199319–20-68) The [M3 90 mm cannon](/wiki/90_mm_Gun_M1/M2/M3 "90 mm Gun M1/M2/M3") used as a towed anti-aircraft and anti-tank gun, and later mounted in the [M36 tank destroyer](/wiki/M36_tank_destroyer "M36 tank destroyer") and finally the late-war [M26 Pershing](/wiki/M26_Pershing "M26 Pershing"), could penetrate the Tiger's front plate at a range of 1,000 m using standard ammunition, and from beyond 2,000 m (6,600 ft) when using HVAP.[[63]](#cite_note-69)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

Soviet ground trial testing conducted in May 1943 determined that the 8.8 cm KwK 36 gun could pierce the T-34/76 frontal beam nose from 1,500 m (4,900 ft), and the front hull from 1500 m. A hit to the driver's hatch would force it to collapse inwards and break apart.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaryatinsky200829–30-70)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKolomiets2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidvS_CAgAAQBAJpgPA92_92]-71)[[g]](#cite_note-72) According to the Wa Prüf 1 report, the Soviet T-34-85's upper glacis and turret front armour would be defeated between 100 and 1,400 m (0.062 and 0.870 mi) at a side angle of 30 deg, while the T-34's 85 mm gun was estimated to penetrate the front of a Tiger between 200 and 500 m (660 and 1,640 ft) at a side angle of 30 degrees to the incoming round.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199319–20-68) Soviet testing showed that the 85 mm gun could penetrate the front of a Tiger from 1,000 m (3,000 ft) with no side angle[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirdLivingston2001[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_January_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (January_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-73)

At a side impact angle of 30 degrees the 120 mm hull armour of the Soviet [IS-2](/wiki/IS-2 "IS-2") model 1943 would be defeated between 100 and 300 m (330 and 980 ft) at the driver's front plate and nose.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199319–20-68) The IS-2's 122 mm gun could penetrate the Tiger's front armour from between 1,500 and 2,500 m (0.93 and 1.55 mi), depending on the impact angle.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199319–20-68) However, according to Steven Zaloga, the IS-2 and Tiger I could each knock the other out in normal combat distances below 1,000 m.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga199412-74) At longer ranges, the performance of each respective tank against each other was dependent on the crew and the combat situation.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga199413-75)

The British [Churchill Mk IV](/wiki/Churchill_tank "Churchill tank") was vulnerable to the Tiger from the front at between 1,100 and 1,700 m (3,600 and 5,600 ft) at a 30 degrees side angle, its strongest point being the nose and its weakest the turret. According to an STT document dated April 1944, it was estimated that the British 76.2 mm [17-pounder](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_17_pounder "Ordnance QF 17 pounder") (used as an anti-tank gun, on the [Sherman Firefly](/wiki/Sherman_Firefly "Sherman Firefly") tank, and self-propelled guns) firing its normal [APCBC](/wiki/APCBC "APCBC") ammunition, would penetrate the turret front and driver's visor plate of the Tiger out to 1,900 yards (1,700 m).[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199319–20-68)

When engaging targets, Tiger crews were encouraged to angle the hull to the 10:30 or 1:30 [clock position](/wiki/Clock_position "Clock position") (45 degrees) relative to the target, an orientation referred to as the *Mahlzeit Stellung*.[[h]](#cite_note-76) This would maximize the effective front hull armour to 180 mm and side hull to 140 mm, making the Tiger impervious to any Allied gun up to 152 mm.[[69]](#cite_note-77)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirdLivingston200183-78) The Tiger's lack of slope for its armour made angling the hull by manual means simple and effective, and unlike the lighter [Panzer IV](/wiki/Panzer_IV "Panzer IV") and [Panther tanks](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank"), the Tiger's thick side armour gave a degree of confidence of immunity from flank attacks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The tank was also immune to Soviet [anti-tank rifle](/wiki/Anti-tank_rifle "Anti-tank rifle") fire to the sides and rear. Its large calibre 8.8 cm provided superior fragmentation and high explosive content over the [7.5 cm KwK 42](/wiki/7.5_cm_KwK_42 "7.5 cm KwK 42") gun. Therefore, comparing the Tiger with the Panther, for supporting the infantry and destroying fortifications, the Tiger offered superior firepower.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

> The destruction of an antitank gun was often accepted as nothing special by lay people and soldiers from other branches. Only the destruction of other tanks counted as a success. On the other hand, antitank guns counted twice as much to the experienced tanker. They were much more dangerous to us. The antitank cannon waited in ambush, well camouflaged, and magnificently set up in the terrain. Because of that, it was very difficult to identify. It was also very difficult to hit because of its low height. Usually, we didn't make out the antitank guns until they had fired the first shot. We were often hit right away, if the antitank crew was on top of things, because we had run into a wall of antitank guns. It was then advisable to keep as cool as possible and take care of the enemy, before the second aimed shot was fired.

— [Otto Carius](/wiki/Otto_Carius "Otto Carius") (translated by Robert J. Edwards), Tigers in the Mud[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarius2003118-79)

### First actions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-554-0872-35%2C_Tunesien%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-554-0872-35%2C_Tunesien%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-554-0872-35,_Tunesien,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I).jpg)

A Tiger I deployed to supplement the [Afrika Korps](/wiki/Afrika_Korps "Afrika Korps") operating in [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"), January 1943

Eager to make use of the powerful new weapon, Hitler ordered the vehicle be pressed into service months earlier than had been planned.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuderian1952280-80) A platoon of four Tigers went into action on 23 September 1942 near [Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShowalter201348-81) Operating in swampy, forested terrain, their movement was largely confined to roads and tracks, making defence against them far easier. Many of these early models were plagued by problems with the transmission, which had difficulty handling the great weight of the vehicle if pushed too hard. It took time for drivers to learn how to avoid overtaxing the engine and transmission, and many broke down. The most significant event from this engagement was that one of the Tigers became stuck in swampy ground and had to be abandoned. Captured largely intact, it enabled the Soviets to study the design and prepare countermeasures.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGlantz2005201-82)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1995335–336-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Niemiecki_czo%C5%82g_%22Tiger%22_na_ulicy_Bolzano_%282-2098%29.jpg/250px-Niemiecki_czo%C5%82g_%22Tiger%22_na_ulicy_Bolzano_%282-2098%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Niemiecki_czo%C5%82g_%22Tiger%22_na_ulicy_Bolzano_(2-2098).jpg)

Tiger I tank on the streets of [Bolzano](/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano") Italy, September 1943

The [503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/503rd_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion") was deployed to the [Don Front](/wiki/Don_Front "Don Front") in the autumn of 1942, but arrived too late to participate in [Operation Winter Storm](/wiki/Operation_Winter_Storm "Operation Winter Storm"), the attempt to relieve Stalingrad. It was subsequently engaged in heavy defensive fighting in the [Rostov-on-Don](/wiki/Rostov-on-Don "Rostov-on-Don") and adjacent sectors in January and February 1943.

In the [North African Campaign](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign"), the Tiger I first saw action during the [Tunisian Campaign](/wiki/Tunisian_Campaign "Tunisian Campaign") on 1 December 1942 east of [Tebourba](/wiki/Tebourba "Tebourba"), when three Tigers attacked an olive grove 5 km (3.1 mi) west of [Djedeida](/wiki/Djedeida "Djedeida").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentz199613-84) The thick olive grove made visibility very limited and enemy tanks were engaged at close range. The Tigers were hit by a number of [M3 Lee](/wiki/M3_Lee "M3 Lee") tanks firing at a range of 80 to 100 metres (260 to 330 ft). Two of the Lees were knocked out in this action, while the Tiger tanks provided effective protection from enemy fire, which greatly increased the crews' trust in the quality of the armour.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentz199613-84) The first loss to an Allied gun was on 20 January 1943 near [Robaa](/w/index.php?title=Robaa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Robaa (page does not exist)"),[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider200443-85) when a battery of the British 72nd Anti-Tank Regiment knocked out a Tiger with their [6-pounder (57 mm) anti-tank guns](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_6_pounder "Ordnance QF 6 pounder"). Seven Tigers were immobilised by mines during the failed attack on [Béja](/wiki/B%C3%A9ja "Béja") during [Operation Ochsenkopf](/wiki/Operation_Ochsenkopf "Operation Ochsenkopf") at the end of February.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider200444-86)

### Later actions

In July 1943, two heavy tank battalions (503rd and 505th) took part in [Operation Citadel](/wiki/Operation_Citadel "Operation Citadel"), including the [Battle of Kursk](/wiki/Battle_of_Kursk "Battle of Kursk"), with one battalion each on the northern (505th) and southern (503rd) flanks of the Kursk [salient](/wiki/Salient_(military) "Salient (military)") the operation was designed to encircle. Although the Tiger often proved dominating, when it saw action, the operation ultimately failed and the Germans were again put on the defensive. The resulting withdrawal led to the loss of many broken-down Tigers which had to be abandoned, with battalions unable to perform required maintenance or repairs.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForty2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidLsxJHQFbPSgCpgPA51_47–50]-87)

On 11 April 1945, a Tiger I destroyed three M4 Sherman tanks and an [armoured car](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") advancing on a road[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*].[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2005-88) On 12 April, a Tiger I (F02) destroyed two Comet tanks, one half-track and one scout car.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2005-88) This Tiger I was destroyed by a Comet tank of A Squadron of the 3rd Royal Tank Regiment on the next day without infantry support.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2005-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-024-3536-28%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Reparatur.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-024-3536-28%2C_Russland%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_I%29%2C_Reparatur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-024-3536-28,_Russland,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_I),_Reparatur.jpg)

A Tiger undergoing engine repairs

The tank's weight significantly limited its use of bridges. For this reason, the Tiger was built with water-tight hatches and a [snorkel](/wiki/Submarine_snorkel "Submarine snorkel") device that allowed it to [ford](/wiki/Ford_(crossing) "Ford (crossing)") water obstacles four metres deep. The tank's weight also made driving through buildings risky, as the presence of a cellar could result in a sudden drop. Due to reliability problems with the Maybach HL 210 TRM P45, which was delivered within the first production batch of 250 Tigers, performance for its maximum power output at high gear ratio could not be fulfilled.[[83]](#cite_note-alanhamby.com-91) Though the Maybach engines had a maximum of 3,000 rpm, crews were told in the *[Tigerfibel](/wiki/Tigerfibel "Tigerfibel")* not to exceed 2,600 rpm. The engine limitation was alleviated only by the adoption of the Maybach HL 230.[[83]](#cite_note-alanhamby.com-91) The turret could also be traversed manually, but this option was rarely used, except when the engine was turned off or sometimes for very small adjustments.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarruthers2013-92)

Early Tigers had a top speed of about 45 km/h (28 mph) over optimal terrain. This was not recommended for normal operation, and was discouraged in training. An engine governor was subsequently installed, capping the engine at 2,600 rpm and the Tiger's maximum speed to about 38 km/h (24 mph). Tiger crews report that typical march speed off-road was 10 km/h (6.2 mph).[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELochmannRubbel200022-93) However, medium tanks of the time, such as the Sherman or T-34, had on average a top speed of about 45 km/h (28 mph). Thus, despite the Tiger being nearly twice as heavy, its speed was comparatively respectable.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarruthers2013-92) With the tank's very wide tracks, a design feature borrowed from the Soviet T-34, the Tiger had a lower ground pressure than many smaller tanks, such as the M4 Sherman.

The first production series Tiger Fgst Nr 250001 with Motor Nr 46052 was only run-in for 25 km (16 mi) by Henschel before being sent to Kummersdorf for testing. During a test drive on 28 May 1942, with only 52 km on the odometer, a blockage occurred in the steering gear. This Tiger quickly went through the original and two replacement engines (Motor Nr 46051 from July 1st to 3rd, Motor Nr 46065 from 6 to 8 July) and was fitted with a fourth motor, Nr 46066, after 13 July. By 3 August 1942, this Tiger had covered a total of 1,046 km (650 mi) by 31 March 1943 a total of 5,623 km (3,494 mi) and by 31 July 1943 a total of 7,736 km (4,807 mi).These figures clearly demonstrate that once the Tiger had overcome its teething troubles, it could withstand a lot of purposefully administered abuse during test programmes.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle199318-94)

The *sPzAbt 501* noted in Combat Report No.6 dated 3 May 1943:

> Regarding the overheating engines, the HL 210 engine caused no troubles during the recent time. All occurring breakdowns resulted from the low quality of driver training. In several cases engine failures have to be put down to the missing remote engine thermometer. Five engines have reached more than 3,000 km (1,900 mi) without essential failures. A good driver is essential for the successful deployment of the Tiger, he must have a good technical training and has to keep his nerve in critical situations…[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201756-95) [[88]](#cite_note-96)

> …The most remarkable aspect of the recent combat was that the Tiger could still be deployed after covering a 400 km (250 mi) run… This proved that the Tiger can easily keep pace with lighter tanks. Nobody expected this.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201761-97)[[90]](#cite_note-98)

The *13.(Tiger) Kompanie/Panzer-Regiment Großdeutschland* reported on their experience in employing the Tiger from 7 to 19 March 1943:

> With the exception of known childhood diseases, the Pz.Kpfw. VI has proven itself to be good. Already, it can be said that its reliability is superior to the Pz.Kpfw.III and IV. When routine care and maintenance are accomplished (that means one day for maintenance for three days in action), even the way it is now, the Tiger can achieve exceptional success.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentz199639-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2926-11A%2C_Russland%2C_Abschleppen_eines_Tiger_I.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2926-11A%2C_Russland%2C_Abschleppen_eines_Tiger_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2926-11A,_Russland,_Abschleppen_eines_Tiger_I.jpg)

Tiger I towed by two [Sd.Kfz. 9](/wiki/Sd.Kfz._9 "Sd.Kfz. 9")

Tiger I tanks needed a high degree of support. It required two or sometimes three of the standard German [Sd.Kfz. 9 *Famo*](/wiki/Sd.Kfz._9 "Sd.Kfz. 9") heavy recovery half-track tractors to tow it. Tiger crews often resorted to using another Tiger to tow the damaged vehicle, but this was not recommended as it often caused overheating and engine breakdown. The low-mounted sprocket limited the obstacle clearance height. The tracks also had a tendency to override the rear sprocket, resulting in immobilisation. If a track overrode and jammed, two Tigers were normally needed to tow the tank. The jammed track was also a big problem itself, since due to high tension, it was often impossible to split the track by removing the track pins. The track sometimes had to be blown apart with a small explosive charge.

The average reliability of the Tiger tank in the second half of 1943 was similar to that of the [Panther](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank"), 36%, compared to the 48% of the [Panzer IV](/wiki/Panzer_IV "Panzer IV") and the 65% of the [StuG III](/wiki/StuG_III "StuG III").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga2015202-100) From May 1944 to March 1945, the reliability of the Tiger tank was comparable to the Panzer IV. With an average of 70%, the Tiger's operational availability on the Western Front, was better than 62% of Panthers. On the Eastern Front, 65% of Tigers were operationally available, compared to the 71% of Panzer IVs and 65% of Panthers.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFletcher2011139-101)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentz1996202,_230-102)

### Tactical organization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-18A%2C_Russland%2C_getarnter_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-18A%2C_Russland%2C_getarnter_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-022-2935-18A,_Russland,_getarnter_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)

A Tiger I camouflaged in a static defensive position

Tigers were usually employed in separate [heavy tank battalions](/wiki/German_Heavy_Panzer_Detachment "German Heavy Panzer Detachment") (*schwere Panzer-Abteilung*) under army command. These battalions would be deployed to critical sectors, either for breakthrough operations or, more typically, counter-attacks. A few favoured divisions, such as the *[Grossdeutschland](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fdeutschland_Division "Großdeutschland Division")*, and the [1st SS *Leibstandarte* Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Leibstandarte "Leibstandarte"), [2nd SS *Das Reich*](/wiki/2nd_SS_Panzer_Division_Das_Reich "2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich"), and [3rd SS *Totenkopf* Panzergrenadier Divisions](/wiki/3rd_SS_Division_Totenkopf "3rd SS Division Totenkopf") at Kursk, had a Tiger company in their tank regiments. The *Grossdeutschland* Division had its Tiger company increased to a battalion as the III Panzer Battalion of the Panzer Regiment *Grossdeutschland*. 3rd SS *Totenkopf* retained its Tiger I company through the entire war. 1st SS and 2nd SS had their Tiger companies taken away and incorporated into the 101st SS Tiger Battalion, which was part of 1st SS Panzer Corps.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilbeck200425,_99-103)

The Tiger was originally designed to be an offensive breakthrough weapon, but by the time it went into action, the military situation had changed dramatically, and its main use was on the defensive, as a mobile anti-tank and infantry gun support weapon.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilbeck200425,_99-103) Tactically, this also meant moving the Tiger units constantly to parry breakthroughs, causing excessive mechanical wear. As a result, Tiger battalions rarely entered combat at full strength.

## Allied response

### British response

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/M26_Killer.jpg/250px-M26_Killer.jpg)](/wiki/File:M26_Killer.jpg)

Tiger I that knocked out the first [M26 Pershing](/wiki/M26_Pershing "M26 Pershing") in combat. It then backed into a pile of rubble and became stuck, leading to the crew abandoning it.

The British had observed the gradual increase in German AFV armour and firepower since 1940 and had anticipated the need for more powerful anti-tank guns. Work on the 76.2 mm calibre [Ordnance QF 17 pounder](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_17_pounder "Ordnance QF 17 pounder") had begun in late 1940 and in 1942 100 early-production guns were rushed to North Africa to help counter the new Tiger threat. The gun carriage had not yet been developed, and the guns were mounted on the carriages of [25-pounder gun-howitzers](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_25_pounder "Ordnance QF 25 pounder") and were known by the code name "Pheasant".

Efforts were hastened to get [cruiser tanks](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank") armed with 17-pounder guns into operation. The [Cruiser Mk VIII Challenger](/wiki/Cruiser_Mk_VIII_Challenger "Cruiser Mk VIII Challenger") (A30) was already at the prototype stage in 1942,[[96]](#cite_note-104) but this tank had relatively weaker armor with a front hull thickness of 64 mm. In the end, it was produced and deployed in limited numbers (around 200 were ordered in 1943), though crews liked it for its high speed. The extemporised [Sherman Firefly](/wiki/Sherman_Firefly "Sherman Firefly"), armed with the 17-pounder, proved to be an excellent anti-tank weapon, but the gun lacked general-purpose capability until later in the war. Fireflies were successfully used against Tigers; in one engagement, a single Firefly destroyed three Tigers in 12 minutes with five rounds.[[97]](#cite_note-105) Sherman Fireflies were deployed one per troop of four 75mm armed standard Shermans. The Germans learned to target Fireflies, so the gun barrel of the Firefly was painted to disguise its length, resembling the M3 75mm gun. This was partially effective.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHart200765-106) Over 2,000 Fireflies were built during the war. Five different 17-pounder-armed British designs saw combat during the war: the Challenger, the [A34 Comet](/wiki/Comet_tank "Comet tank") (using the related [QF 77mm HV](/wiki/OQF_77mm_HV "OQF 77mm HV")), the Sherman Firefly, the [17pdr SP Achilles](/wiki/17pdr_SP_Achilles "17pdr SP Achilles") (up-gunned M10 GMC), and the [17pdr SP Archer](/wiki/Archer_(tank_destroyer) "Archer (tank destroyer)") self-propelled gun, while one more, the [A41 Centurion](/wiki/Centurion_Tank "Centurion Tank"), was about to enter service as the European war ended. In 1944 the British introduced an [APDS](/wiki/Armour-piercing_discarding_sabot "Armour-piercing discarding sabot") round for the 17-pounder, which increased penetration performance considerably.

### Soviet response

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Zhukov_at_the_Tiger_tank.jpg/250px-Zhukov_at_the_Tiger_tank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhukov_at_the_Tiger_tank.jpg)

Marshal [Georgy Zhukov](/wiki/Georgy_Zhukov "Georgy Zhukov") inspecting a Tiger captured by the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") in 1943

The first Tiger tank was captured by Soviet troops in January 1943 on the [Volkhov Front](/wiki/Volkhov_Front "Volkhov Front").[[99]](#cite_note-107)

Initially, the Soviets responded to the Tiger I by restarting production of the [57 mm ZiS-2](/wiki/57_mm_anti-tank_gun_M1943_(ZiS-2) "57 mm anti-tank gun M1943 (ZiS-2)") anti-tank gun (production was stopped in 1941 in favour of cheaper and more versatile alternatives – e.g. the [ZiS-3](/wiki/ZiS-3 "ZiS-3") – as the gun's performance was excessive for early German armour). The ZiS-2 had better armour penetration than the 76 mm [F-34 tank gun](/wiki/F-34_tank_gun "F-34 tank gun") used by most Red Army tanks, or the ZiS-3 76 mm divisional cannon, but was still inadequate against Tigers. A small number of T-34s were again fitted with a tank version of the ZiS-2, the ZiS-4, but it could not fire an adequate high-explosive round, making it an unsuitable tank gun.

Firing trials of the new [85 mm D-5T](/wiki/85_mm_air_defense_gun_M1939_(52-K) "85 mm air defense gun M1939 (52-K)") also had proved disappointing. Several captured German Tiger I tanks were shipped to Chelyabinsk, where they were subjected to 85 mm fire from various angles. The 85 mm gun could not reliably penetrate the Tiger I's armour except at ranges within the lethal envelope of the Tiger I's own 88 mm gun.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga19946–7-108) It was still initially used on the [SU-85](/wiki/SU-85 "SU-85") self-propelled gun (based on a T-34 chassis) from August 1943. The production of KV heavy tanks armed with the 85 mm D-5T in an IS-85 turret was also started. There was a short production run of 148 KV-85 tanks, which were sent to the front beginning in September 1943 with production ending by December 1943.[[101]](#cite_note-109) By early 1944, the T-34/85 appeared; this up-gunned T-34 matched the SU-85's firepower, but with the advantage of mounting the gun in a turret. It also matched the firepower of the heavier IS-85 tank in a more cost-effective package, resulting in a repetition of the events which heralded the decline of KV-1 production. The IS was subsequently rearmed with the 122 mm D-25T, which with BR–471 AP rounds was capable of punching through the Tiger's armour from 1,200 m,[[102]](#cite_note-110) and with the improved BR–471B APHEBC rounds at over 2,000 m.[[103]](#cite_note-111)[[i]](#cite_note-113) The redundant SU-85 was replaced by the [SU-100](/wiki/SU-100 "SU-100"), mounting a 100 mm [D-10 tank gun](/wiki/D-10_tank_gun "D-10 tank gun"), that could penetrate 149 mm of vertical armour plate at 1,000 m.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga1994225-114)

In May 1943, the Red Army deployed the [SU-152](/wiki/SU-152 "SU-152"), which was replaced in 1944 by the [ISU-152](/wiki/ISU-152 "ISU-152"). Both these self-propelled guns mounted the large [152 mm howitzer-gun](/wiki/152_mm_howitzer-gun_M1937_(ML-20) "152 mm howitzer-gun M1937 (ML-20)"). The SU-152 was intended to be a close-support gun for use against German fortifications rather than armour; however, it shared with the later fielded ISU-152 the nickname *Zveroboy* ("beast killer") for its rare ability to knock out German heavy tanks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*] The 152 mm armour-piercing shells weighed over 45 kilograms (99 lb) and could penetrate a Tiger's frontal armour from about 1,000 metres (1,100 yd). Its high-explosive rounds would destroy anything that was attached to the outside of the tank, and had a knack for immobilizing any tank it hit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The sheer force of the shell meant that at certain points it could knock off the turrets of the Tiger series. However, the size and weight of the ammunition meant both vehicles had a low rate of fire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*]

### US response

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/TigerITankTunis.jpg/250px-TigerITankTunis.jpg)](/wiki/File:TigerITankTunis.jpg)

Tiger 712 captured by US forces in [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis"), 1943.

The US Army hesitated to place 76 mm M1 guns in action even when they were already available, as combat through early 1944 indicated that the 75 mm M3 was more than adequate for handling the German tank threat.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga200313-115) This conclusion was partly based on the correct estimate that Tigers would be encountered in relatively small numbers, and on the assumption that anti-tank gun-fire (as in Tunisia and Sicily) rather than tanks could knock them out.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaloga200314-116) But as early as 1942, the US was already planning and testing the rearming of the M4 Sherman with the 76mm gun, expecting to encounter heavier German armor later in the war.

## Surviving vehicles

### Running

Main article: [Tiger 131](/wiki/Tiger_131 "Tiger 131")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Tiger_131_%287527948486%29.jpg/250px-Tiger_131_%287527948486%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiger_131_(7527948486).jpg)

[Tiger 131](/wiki/Tiger_131 "Tiger 131"), Bovington Tank Museum, United Kingdom

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/The_British_Army_in_Tunisia_1943_NA3693.jpg/250px-The_British_Army_in_Tunisia_1943_NA3693.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_British_Army_in_Tunisia_1943_NA3693.jpg)

[King George VI](/wiki/King_George_VI "King George VI") inspecting Tiger 131 in Tunis, June 1943. The badge of the [British First Army](/wiki/First_Army_(United_Kingdom) "First Army (United Kingdom)") had been painted onto the tank.

On 24 April 1943, a Tiger I of the 504th [German heavy tank battalion](/wiki/German_heavy_tank_battalion "German heavy tank battalion"), with [turret number 131](/wiki/Tiger_131 "Tiger 131"), was captured at Point 174 (Gueriat el Atach) in [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"). Earlier accounts stated that the tank had been disabled at Djebel Djaffa on 21 April 1943 by [6-pounder](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_6_pounder "Ordnance QF 6 pounder") solid shot fired from a [Churchill tank](/wiki/Churchill_tank "Churchill tank") of the British [48th Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/48th_Royal_Tank_Regiment "48th Royal Tank Regiment"), which ricocheted from the Tiger's gun barrel into the turret ring, jamming the turret traverse and forcing the crew to abandon the vehicle.[[112]](#cite_note-:1-121)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarruthers2000-122)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] However, research published by [The Tank Museum](/wiki/The_Tank_Museum "The Tank Museum") in 2019 concluded that Tiger 131 was actually disabled three days later, on 24 April 1943, at Point 174 (Gueriat el Atach) during fighting involving the [2nd Battalion Sherwood Foresters](/wiki/2nd_Battalion_Sherwood_Foresters "2nd Battalion Sherwood Foresters") and supporting Churchill tanks. The turret was jammed by a 6-pounder round fired from one of the supporting Churchill tanks,[[j]](#cite_note-123) causing the crew to abandon the vehicle, which was subsequently captured by British forces.[[112]](#cite_note-:1-121)[[114]](#cite_note-124) After repairs, the tank was sent to England for a thorough inspection.

The captured tank was officially handed over to the [Bovington Tank Museum](/wiki/Bovington_Tank_Museum "Bovington Tank Museum") by the British [Ministry of Supply](/wiki/Ministry_of_Supply_(UK) "Ministry of Supply (UK)") on 25 September 1951. In June 1990, the tank was removed from display at the museum and work began on its restoration. This was carried out both by the museum and the [Army Base Repair Organisation](/wiki/Army_Base_Repair_Organisation "Army Base Repair Organisation") and involved an almost complete disassembly of the tank. The Maybach HL230 engine from the museum's Tiger II was installed (the Tiger's original Maybach HL210 had been sectioned for display[[115]](#cite_note-125)), along with a modern fire-suppressant system in the engine compartment. In December 2003, Tiger 131 returned to the museum, restored and in running condition. Tiger 131 was used in the film [*Fury*](/wiki/Fury_(2014_film) "Fury (2014 film)"), the first time an original, fully mechanically operable Tiger I appeared in a movie since World War II.[[116]](#cite_note-126) The fire suppression system was removed as it interfered with engine maintenance and was too obtrusive.[[117]](#cite_note-127)

### Others

Given the low number of just over 1,300 Tiger Is produced during World War II, very few survived the war and the subsequent post-war scrapping drives. According to the memoirs of the veterans of the Kubinka training ground, dozens of captured Tigers were used in the USSR as targets in the 50s, and then were sent to the Stalingrad plant for smelting.[[118]](#cite_note-128) The Tiger, preserved in the Lenino-Snegiri Museum, was used as a target at the Nakhabino engineering range and survived. Many large components have been salvaged over the years, but the discovery of a more or less and generally complete vehicle has so far eluded armour enthusiasts and tank collectors. In addition to Tiger 131, nine other Tiger I tanks survive:

* [Musée des Blindés](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_des_Blind%C3%A9s "Musée des Blindés") in [Saumur](/wiki/Saumur "Saumur"), [France](/wiki/France "France"). Indoor exhibit in good condition. Mid-production (1944) version with overlapping 'steel'-type roadwheels adopted from the Tiger II and fitted with the narrow transport tracks. This Tiger was part of the 2nd company of the [SS Heavy Panzer Battalion 102](/wiki/SS_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion_102 "SS Heavy Panzer Battalion 102") which fought in the [Cauville](/wiki/Cauville "Cauville") sector and was later abandoned by her crew after a mechanical breakdown. She was recommissioned as *Colmar* with the 2nd squadron of the Free French [6th Cuirassier Regiment](/wiki/6th_Cuirassier_Regiment_(France) "6th Cuirassier Regiment (France)") and joined the new unit in fighting all the way back to Germany.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In late 2023, the museum initiated a crowdfunding campaign[[119]](#cite_note-129) to restore it to running status. As the crowdfunding campaign was successful, the tank is now, as of early 2024, under restoration by the museum and is expected to return to running status soon.[[120]](#cite_note-130)
* [Vimoutiers](/wiki/Vimoutiers "Vimoutiers") in [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), France. The renowned "[Vimoutiers Tiger tank](/wiki/Vimoutiers_Tiger_tank "Vimoutiers Tiger tank")". Abandoned and then destroyed (to prevent enemy capture) by its German crew in August 1944. An outdoor monument in poor condition due to the effect of time and the elements (many original parts such as hatches and both rear exhaust pipes missing).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Kubinka Tank Museum](/wiki/Kubinka_Tank_Museum "Kubinka Tank Museum") in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). In good condition; displayed as an indoor exhibit (although the outermost row of four roadwheels is missing on this vehicle).
* Military-Historical Museum of Lenino-Snegiri in Russia. In very bad condition; displayed outdoors. This tank was a former Nakhabino firing-range target in and has been badly shot-at and cut up (damage includes broken running gear and multiple shell-holes in its armour). The tank received additional damage during improper transportation from Moscow to Saratov on the Volga and back, losing some of its parts. Saved pictures of the condition before it was transported [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Tiger 712 (hull number 250031) of the [501st Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/501st_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "501st Heavy Panzer Battalion") was captured in North Africa in May 1943 and is a part of the [United States Army Armor & Cavalry Collection](/wiki/United_States_Army_Armor_%26_Cavalry_Collection "United States Army Armor & Cavalry Collection"), [Fort Benning](/wiki/Fort_Benning "Fort Benning"), Georgia. In good condition; formerly displayed outdoors, it has since been moved indoors. This vehicle had its left turret and upper-hull sides partially cutaway in 1946 for instructional and display purposes.[[121]](#cite_note-131)
* Mr Hoebig, a private collector – Long Island, New York, United States. The "Hoebig Tiger" (marked as 231) is a Tiger reconstructed by Hoebig from various components sourced from the Trun Scrapyard in Normandy,[[122]](#cite_note-132) with the wheels and gun coming from Kurland (in Latvia). Prior to December 2016, it was on display at the [German Panzer Museum](/wiki/German_Tank_Museum "German Tank Museum") in [Munster](/wiki/Munster,_Lower_Saxony "Munster, Lower Saxony").[[123]](#cite_note-133)
* "Hoebig Second Tiger". Put together from a Russian scrapped chassis and various other found parts. This is non-running late model Tiger, the second built by Hoebig, construction starting in 2012. It was first displayed at Militracks in 2023.[[124]](#cite_note-134)
* [Australian Armour and Artillery Museum](/wiki/Australian_Armour_and_Artillery_Museum "Australian Armour and Artillery Museum"). This Tiger, restored in 2021, is an externally complete Tiger using battlefield relics and a proportionally small amount of replica plate/components, similar to the Hoebig Tigers and Wheatcroft Tigers[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [The Wheatcroft Collection](/wiki/The_Wheatcroft_Collection "The Wheatcroft Collection"). Has a significant number of components to assemble two Tigers made from a mixture of new replacement and original Tiger pieces. The re-construction of one stalled several years ago, with little information coming from the collection with regard to the progress of the project. Controversially, [Kevin Wheatcroft](/wiki/Kevin_Wheatcroft "Kevin Wheatcroft") has maintained that the restorations would use 100% original components and parts; however, photos obtained within "the secure zone" (Wheatcroft safe storage and workshop facility) show clearly that more than 60% of the upper deck and the entire engine area have been fabricated from new materials.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By test firing the 8.8 cm gun against a T-34 hull, data was obtained that led to several improvements of the T-34 and development of the IS II as a new breakthrough tank. By increasing the thickness of the armour and mounting it with a very heavy 122 mm gun, the Soviet IS II became a very difficult tank to deal with.
